Marks a conditional activation gate within a group.
Lookahead: 2
{mcm_attribute_reference_s}
— the enumerated variable (voicing
{axt_enumeration})
and the specific
{axt_enum_value}
that activates this gate.
The gate variable may belong to a different regime (cross-regime gating is legal).

[[axhrgv_variable]]
{axhrgv_variable}::
Marks a variable within the current group in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the variable being detailed within the current group scope.

[[axhro_kindle]]
{axhro_kindle}::
Marks a regime kindle operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level kindle operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_kindle}
in the parent document).

[[axhro_validate]]
{axhro_validate}::
Marks a regime validate operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level validate operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_validate}
in the parent document).

[[axhro_render]]
{axhro_render}::
Marks a regime render operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level render operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_render}
in the parent document).

[[axhro_list]]
{axhro_list}::
Marks a regime list operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level list operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_list}
in the parent document).
Only valid on
{axrd_manifold}
regimes.

[[axhro_survey]]
{axhro_survey}::
Marks a regime survey operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level survey operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_survey}
in the parent document).
Only valid on
{axrd_manifold}
regimes.

[[axhro_audit]]
{axhro_audit}::
Marks a regime audit operation description in a subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the kit-level audit operation term (which must be voiced
{axvr_audit}
in the parent document).
Only valid on
{axrd_manifold}
regimes.

==== Nesting Rules

Hierarchy markers establish implicit scope through document order:

* Higher-level markers implicitly close previous scope (no end markers needed)
* {axhrgb_group},
{axhrgc_gate},
and
{axhrgv_variable}
must appear within
{axhrb_regime}
scope
* {axhrgc_gate}
and
{axhrgv_variable}
must appear within
{axhrgb_group}
scope
* Multiple
{axhrgc_gate}
within one group have AND semantics
* {axhro_kindle},
{axhro_validate},
{axhro_render},
{axhro_list},
{axhro_survey},
and
{axhro_audit}
must appear within
{axhrb_regime}
scope but outside any
{axhrgb_group}
scope

==== Cross-Document Validation

* {axhrb_regime}
attribute reference must match a regime defined with
{axvr_regime}
in parent
* {axhrgb_group}
attribute reference must match a group defined with
{axvr_group}
under that regime
* {axhrgc_gate}
first attribute reference must voice
{axt_enumeration};
second must be an
{axt_enum_value}
of that specific enumeration type

== Axial Lexicon Structural Terms

[[axl_definition_site]]
{axl_definition_site}::
The location in a parent document where a linked term is defined: anchor, voicing annotation, and definition text.
The
{axl_definition_site}
establishes the term's identity, role, and architectural relationships.
Voicing annotations at the
{axl_definition_site}
may impose positional requirements on linked terms in the definition text that follows.

[[axl_detail_site]]
{axl_detail_site}::
The location where a linked term's details are elaborated through hierarchy markers and prose.
Typically a subdocument included from the parent, but may be inline within the parent document for compact definitions.
The
{axl_detail_site}
is opened by a hierarchy block marker (e.g.,
{axhob_operation},
{axhrb_regime})
and contains subordinate markers for parameters, steps, variables, or other structural elements.

content.content:
2797	== Axial Hierarchy Protocol Markers
2798	
2799	{axhp*} markers appear at the
2800	{axl_detail_site}
2801	of an
2802	{axi_protocol}
2803	as standalone annotations, paralleling the
2804	{axho*}
2805	operation hierarchy markers.
2806	A protocol's structural detail — the roster of transactions, and within each transaction the call envelope and return envelope — is expressed through a four-level nesting of these markers.
2807	
2808	=== Marker Terms and Lookahead Arities
2809	
2810	[cols="<,^,<",options="header"]
2811	|===
2812	|Marker |Lookahead |Reads
2813	
2814	|{axhpb_protocol}   |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The protocol this detail site describes (no dimensions — dimensions are at {axl_definition_site} only)
2815	|{axhpt_transaction}|0                            |A named call/return pairing within the protocol; bare marker, identity carried through heading and prose
2816	|{axhpc_call}       |0                            |The caller-side envelope of an enclosing transaction; bare marker, members listed in following prose
2817	|{axhpr_return}     |0                            |The callee-side envelope of an enclosing transaction; bare marker, members listed in following prose
2818	|===
2819	
2820	[[axhpb_protocol]]
2821	{axhpb_protocol}::
2822	Opens the protocol hierarchy block at the
2823	{axl_detail_site}.
2824	Lookahead: 1
2825	{mcm_attribute_reference}
2826	— the protocol this detail site describes.
2827	The referenced attribute must correspond to an anchor annotated with
2828	{axi_protocol}
2829	at the
2830	{axl_definition_site}.
2831	No dimensions on this marker — all dimensions (transport, lifecycle, concurrency) are authoritative at the
2832	{axl_definition_site}
2833	only.
2834	
2835	The surface syntax:
2836	
2837	[source,asciidoc]
2838	----
2839	//axhpb_protocol
2840	{apcswp_wire}
2841	
2842	//axhpt_transaction
2843	discerner — main detection transaction.
2844	
2845	//axhpc_call
2846	caller payload members:
2847	----
2848	
2849	[[axhpt_transaction]]
2850	{axhpt_transaction}::
2851	Marks a named call/return pairing within an
2852	{axi_protocol}
2853	detail site.
2854	No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
2855	A transaction groups exactly one
2856	{axhpc_call}
2857	and one
2858	{axhpr_return}
2859	that together constitute a single caller-initiated exchange.
2860	Transaction identity is carried through section heading and prose; no linked term is expected on the following line.
2861	Multiple
2862	{axhpt_transaction}
2863	blocks within one protocol describe the full set of exchanges the protocol supports.
2864	
2865	[[axhpc_call]]
2866	{axhpc_call}::
2867	Marks the caller-side envelope of an enclosing
2868	{axhpt_transaction}.
2869	No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
2870	The prose under this marker enumerates the envelope's members, each referencing a type term defined at the
2871	{axl_definition_site}
2872	(in the parent spec or a shared vocabulary).
2873	Exactly one
2874	{axhpc_call}
2875	per
2876	{axhpt_transaction}.
2877	
2878	[[axhpr_return]]
2879	{axhpr_return}::
2880	Marks the callee-side envelope of an enclosing
2881	{axhpt_transaction}.
2882	No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
2883	The prose under this marker enumerates the envelope's members using the same type-reference pattern as
2884	{axhpc_call}.
2885	Exactly one
2886	{axhpr_return}
2887	per
2888	{axhpt_transaction}.
2889	A transaction where the caller expects no data back still declares
2890	{axhpr_return}
2891	(with prose noting "empty envelope" or equivalent) — the return presence is the signal, and uniform structure keeps the hierarchy complete.
2892	
2893	=== Nesting Rules
2894	
2895	Protocol hierarchy markers establish implicit scope through document order:
2896	
2897	* {axhpb_protocol}
2898	opens the protocol block; all subsequent markers belong to it until document end or next
2899	{axhpb_protocol}
2900	* {axhpt_transaction}
2901	must appear within
2902	{axhpb_protocol}
2903	scope
2904	* {axhpc_call}
2905	and
2906	{axhpr_return}
2907	must appear within
2908	{axhpt_transaction}
2909	scope, each exactly once
2910	* Transactions appear in document order; no required ordering is imposed, but convention places readiness or probe transactions first and main-workload transactions second
2911	
2912	=== Cross-Document Validation
2913	
2914	* {axhpb_protocol}
2915	attribute reference must match a protocol defined with
2916	{axi_protocol}
2917	at the
2918	{axl_definition_site}
2919	* {axhpb_protocol}
2920	carries no dimensions — all dimensions are authoritative at the
2921	{axl_definition_site}
2922	only
2923	* Types referenced in
2924	{axhpc_call}
2925	and
2926	{axhpr_return}
2927	member prose must resolve to
2928	{axt_type}
2929	anchors in the parent spec or a shared vocabulary
2930	
2931	== Axial Voicing Entity Terms
2932	
2933	Three annotation families enable entity specification across
2934	{axl_definition_site_s}
2935	and
2936	{axl_detail_site_s},
2937	paralleling the regime and operation annotation patterns.
2938	
2939	Entity specifications describe objects with both structure (fields) and behavior (methods).
2940	Methods within an entity may be *scoped* or *elevated*:
2941	
2942	* A *scoped* method is defined entirely within the entity subdocument.
2943	It has no parent-document linked term — its identity exists only within the entity's contract, expressed through position and prose.
2944	
2945	* An *elevated* method possesses a linked term at the
2946	{axl_definition_site}
2947	in the parent document, voiced with
2948	{axvo_method}.
2949	Elevated methods earn independent addressability when other spec layers (procedures, dispatch protocols) need to reference them.
2950	
2951	A well-constrained entity is its own documentation.
2952	Scoped methods are the default; elevation is the exception for methods that must be independently addressable.
2953	
2954	=== Definition-Site Voicing Annotations (axve_*)
2955	
2956	{axve_*} annotations appear at the
2957	{axl_definition_site}
2958	between anchor and definition, declaring that the term defines an entity.
2959	
2960	[[axve_entity]]
2961	{axve_entity}::
2962	Voices an
2963	{axo_entity}
2964	definition.
2965	The annotated term defines an object with both structure and behavior whose contract is elaborated in a subdocument via
2966	{axhe*}
2967	hierarchy markers.
2968	The first
2969	{mcm_attribute_reference}
2970	is the entity being defined.
2971	+
2972	{axd_dimension_s}:
2973	optional
2974	{axd_internal}.
2975	
2976	=== Standalone Hierarchy Markers (axhe*_)
2977	
2978	Hierarchy markers appear in entity subdocuments as standalone annotations, NOT between anchor and definition.
2979	Each marker uses the prefix-discriminated form.
2980	Markers with non-zero arity read ahead a specified number of
2981	{mcm_attribute_reference_s}
2982	from the following text.
2983	Zero-arity markers carry their type and dimension information entirely on the annotation line.
2984	
2985	The entity hierarchy uses a two-layer structure:
2986	
2987	* *Layer 1*: Entity scope opened by
2988	{axheb_entity},
2989	containing field markers
2990	({axheft_typed_field},
2991	{axhefm_motif_field})
2992	and method markers
2993	({axhems_scoped_method},
2994	{axheme_elevated_method}).
2995	
2996	* *Layer 2*: Method scope opened by a method marker, containing parameter markers
2997	({axhempt_typed_parameter},
2998	{axhempa_arg_parameter},
2999	{axhempm_motif_parameter})
3000	and output markers
3001	({axhemot_typed_output},
3002	{axhemom_motif_output}).
3003	
3004	Variant suffixes identify the source of type identity:
3005	
3006	* `_typed_*` — 1-arity; lookahead reads a project-specific type term
3007	* `_arg_*` — 1-arity; lookahead reads a shared argument term
3008	* `_motif_*` — 0-arity; type expressed through axial motif terms on the annotation line
3009	
3010	==== Marker Terms and Lookahead Arities
3011	
3012	[cols="<,^,<",options="header"]
3013	|===
3014	|Marker |Lookahead |Reads
3015	
3016	|{axheb_entity}             |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The entity this subdoc describes
3017	|{axheft_typed_field}       |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A field's type (domain type term)
3018	|{axhefm_motif_field}       |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
3019	|{axhems_scoped_method}     |1 {mcm_inlay}                |Implementation method name; opens Layer 2
3020	|{axheme_elevated_method}   |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The parent-document method term
3021	|{axhempt_typed_parameter}  |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A parameter's type (domain type term)
3022	|{axhempa_arg_parameter}    |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A parameter's identity (shared argument term)
3023	|{axhempm_motif_parameter}  |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
3024	|{axhemot_typed_output}     |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |An output's type (domain type term)
3025	|{axhemom_motif_output}     |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
3026	|===
3027	
3028	[[axheb_entity]]
3029	{axheb_entity}::
3030	Opens the entity hierarchy block at the
3031	{axl_detail_site}.
3032	Lookahead: 1
3033	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3034	— the entity this detail site describes.
3035	The referenced attribute must correspond to an anchor annotated with
3036	{axve_entity}
3037	at the
3038	{axl_definition_site}.
3039	
3040	[[axheft_typed_field]]
3041	{axheft_typed_field}::
3042	Marks a data member of the entity whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
3043	Lookahead: 1
3044	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3045	— must resolve to an existing type anchor.
3046	{axd_dimension_s}
3047	on the annotation line qualify the field:
3048	{axd_required}
3049	or
3050	{axd_optional},
3051	{axd_repeated},
3052	etc.
3053	
3054	[[axhefm_motif_field]]
3055	{axhefm_motif_field}::
3056	Marks a data member of the entity whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
3057	0-arity — no lookahead.
3058	{axd_dimension_s}
3059	on the annotation line include a type motif
3060	({axt_string},
3061	{axt_boolean},
3062	{axt_array},
3063	etc.) and structural qualifiers
3064	({axd_required},
3065	{axd_repeated},
3066	etc.).
3067	
3068	[[axhems_scoped_method]]
3069	{axhems_scoped_method}::
3070	Opens a scoped method scope within the entity.
3071	The method has no parent-document
3072	{mcm_quoin}
3073	— its identity is an
3074	{mcm_inlay}
3075	that exists only within the entity subdocument.
3076	Lookahead: 1
3077	{mcm_inlay}
3078	— the implementation method name, recognized by its prefix pattern.
3079	Opens Layer 2 scope for parameter and output markers.
3080	
3081	[[axheme_elevated_method]]
3082	{axheme_elevated_method}::
3083	References an elevated method within the entity — a method that has a linked term at the
3084	{axl_definition_site}
3085	in the parent document, annotated with
3086	{axvo_method}.
3087	Lookahead: 1
3088	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3089	— the parent-document method term.
3090	Opens Layer 2 scope for parameter and output markers.
3091	
3092	[[axhempt_typed_parameter]]
3093	{axhempt_typed_parameter}::
3094	Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
3095	Lookahead: 1
3096	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3097	— must resolve to an existing type anchor.
3098	{axd_dimension_s}
3099	on the annotation line qualify the parameter:
3100	{axd_required}
3101	or
3102	{axd_optional},
3103	etc.
3104	
3105	[[axhempa_arg_parameter]]
3106	{axhempa_arg_parameter}::
3107	Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose identity reuses a shared argument term.
3108	Lookahead: 1
3109	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3110	— must resolve to an existing shared argument anchor.
3111	
3112	[[axhempm_motif_parameter]]
3113	{axhempm_motif_parameter}::
3114	Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
3115	0-arity — no lookahead.
3116	{axd_dimension_s}
3117	on the annotation line include a type motif and structural qualifiers.
3118	
3119	[[axhemot_typed_output]]
3120	{axhemot_typed_output}::
3121	Marks an output of the current method scope whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
3122	Lookahead: 1
3123	{mcm_attribute_reference}
3124	— must resolve to an existing type anchor.
3125	
3126	[[axhemom_motif_output]]
3127	{axhemom_motif_output}::
3128	Marks an output of the current method scope whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
3129	0-arity — no lookahead.
3130	{axd_dimension_s}
3131	on the annotation line include a type motif and structural qualifiers.
3132	
3133	==== Nesting Rules
3134	
3135	Entity hierarchy markers establish implicit scope through document order:
3136	
3137	* {axheb_entity}
3138	opens the entity block; all subsequent markers belong to it until document end
3139	* {axheft_typed_field}
3140	and
3141	{axhefm_motif_field}
3142	must appear within
3143	{axheb_entity}
3144	scope but outside any method scope (Layer 1)
3145	* {axhems_scoped_method}
3146	and
3147	{axheme_elevated_method}
3148	must appear within
3149	{axheb_entity}
3150	scope (Layer 1); each opens a new method scope (Layer 2)
3151	* A method marker implicitly closes any prior method scope
3152	* {axhempt_typed_parameter},
3153	{axhempa_arg_parameter},
3154	{axhempm_motif_parameter},
3155	{axhemot_typed_output},
3156	and
3157	{axhemom_motif_output}
3158	must appear within a method scope (Layer 2)
3159	* Field markers should precede method markers within the entity (fields then methods)
3160	
3161	==== Cross-Document Validation
3162	
3163	* {axheb_entity}
3164	attribute reference must match an entity defined with
3165	{axve_entity}
3166	at the
3167	{axl_definition_site}
3168	* {axheme_elevated_method}
3169	attribute reference must match a method defined with
3170	{axvo_method}
3171	at the
3172	{axl_definition_site},
3173	and that method's affiliated entity must match the entity opened by
3174	{axheb_entity}
3175	
3176	== Axial Type Universal Terms
3177	
3178	{axtu_*} types represent generic infrastructure patterns that are not specific to any cloud provider or platform.
3179	These types capture critical gestalts that appear across many systems.
3180	Platform-specific nuances (e.g., path separators on Windows vs Unix) are handled through
3181	{axl_voicing_s}.
3182	
3183	[[axtu_string]]
3184	{axtu_string}::
3185	A
3186	{axl_motif}
3187	representing a generic bounded string value.
3188	{axl_voicing_s}
3189	specify minimum and maximum length constraints appropriate to their context.
3190	
3191	[[axtu_path]]
3192	{axtu_path}::
3193	A
3194	{axl_motif}
3195	representing a file system path.
3196	Platform differences (path separators, root conventions) are handled by
3197	{axl_voicing_s}.
3198	May be absolute or relative depending on context.
3199	
3200	[[axtu_ipv4]]
3201	{axtu_ipv4}::
3202	A
3203	{axl_motif}
3204	representing an IPv4 address in dotted-decimal notation.
3205	Format: four octets 0-255 separated by dots (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).
3206	
3207	[[axtu_cidr]]
3208	{axtu_cidr}::
3209	A
3210	{axl_motif}
3211	representing a CIDR block for network addressing.
3212	Format: IPv4 address followed by slash and prefix length (e.g., `10.0.0.0/8`).
3213	
3214	[[axtu_port]]
3215	{axtu_port}::
3216	A
3217	{axl_motif}
3218	representing a network port number.
3219	Valid range: 1-65535. Well-known ports are 1-1023.
3220	
3221	[[axtu_domain]]
3222	{axtu_domain}::
3223	A
3224	{axl_motif}
3225	representing a DNS domain name.
3226	Must start and end with alphanumeric characters; may contain dots and hyphens.
3227	
3228	[[axtu_sha256]]
3229	{axtu_sha256}::
3230	A
3231	{axl_motif}
3232	representing a SHA-256 cryptographic hash.
3233	Format: 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters.
3234	
3235	[[axtu_xname]]
3236	{axtu_xname}::
3237	A
3238	{axl_motif}
3239	representing a cross-context safe identifier.
3240	Must start with a letter and contain only letters, digits, underscores, and hyphens.
3241	Safe for use in file names, environment variables, and most programming contexts.
3242	
3243	== Axial Type Google Terms
3244	
3245	{axtg_*} types represent Google Cloud Platform resource identifiers and patterns.
3246	These types have specific format requirements enforced by GCP APIs.
3247	
3248	[[axtg_project_id]]
3249	{axtg_project_id}::
3250	A
3251	{axl_motif}
3252	representing a Google Cloud Platform project identifier.
3253	Format: 6-30 lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens; must start with a letter and end with letter or digit.
3254	Globally unique across all of GCP.
3255	
3256	[[axtg_region]]
3257	{axtg_region}::
3258	A
3259	{axl_motif}
3260	representing a Google Cloud Platform region code.
3261	Format: lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens (e.g., `us-central1`, `europe-west1`).
3262	Determines geographic location for regional resources.
3263	
3264	[[axtg_service_account]]
3265	{axtg_service_account}::
3266	A
3267	{axl_motif}
3268	representing a Google Cloud Platform service account email.
3269	Format: `{account-id}@{project-id}.iam.gserviceaccount.com` where account-id is 6-30 characters.
3270	Used as identity for programmatic API access.
3271	
3272	[[axtg_billing_account]]
3273	{axtg_billing_account}::
3274	A
3275	{axl_motif}
3276	representing a Google Cloud Platform billing account identifier.
3277	Format: `XXXXXX-XXXXXX-XXXXXX` (three groups of six uppercase alphanumeric characters).
3278	Links projects to payment sources.
3279	
3280	[[axtg_project_number]]
3281	{axtg_project_number}::
3282	A
3283	{axl_motif}
3284	representing a Google Cloud Platform project number.
3285	Format: numeric identifier automatically assigned at project creation.
3286	Globally unique across all of GCP.
3287	Immutable once assigned.
3288	
3289	== Axial Infrastructure Google Terms
3290	
3291	{axig_*} terms represent Google Cloud Platform infrastructure patterns.
3292	Each term explicitly declares its level:
3293	
3294	* **Service-level**: the managed platform itself
3295	* **Instance-level**: a particular resource within a service
3296	* **Operational**: execution patterns and states
3297	
3298	=== Service-Level Terms
3299	
3300	[[axig_resource_manager]]
3301	{axig_resource_manager}::
3302	A
3303	{axl_motif}
3304	representing Google Cloud Resource Manager.
3305	Service-level: the platform for managing organizations, folders, and projects.
3306	
3307	[[axig_cloud_storage]]
3308	{axig_cloud_storage}::
3309	A
3310	{axl_motif}
3311	representing Google Cloud Storage.
3312	Service-level: the object storage platform, not buckets within it.
3313	
3314	[[axig_artifact_registry]]
3315	{axig_artifact_registry}::
3316	A
3317	{axl_motif}
3318	representing Google Artifact Registry.
3319	Service-level: the artifact management platform, not repositories within it.
3320	
3321	[[axig_cloud_build]]
3322	{axig_cloud_build}::
3323	A
3324	{axl_motif}
3325	representing Google Cloud Build.
3326	Service-level: the build execution platform, not individual builds.
3327	
3328	[[axig_iam]]
3329	{axig_iam}::
3330	A
3331	{axl_motif}
3332	representing Google Cloud IAM.
3333	Service-level: the identity and access management platform.
3334	
3335	=== Instance-Level: Resource Manager
3336	
3337	[[axig_organization]]
3338	{axig_organization}::
3339	A
3340	{axl_motif}
3341	representing a Google Cloud organization.
3342	Instance-level: a particular organization, top of GCP resource hierarchy.
3343	
3344	[[axig_folder]]
3345	{axig_folder}::
3346	A
3347	{axl_motif}
3348	representing a Google Cloud folder.
3349	Instance-level: an organizational container within an
3350	{axig_organization}.
3351	
3352	[[axig_project]]
3353	{axig_project}::
3354	A
3355	{axl_motif}
3356	representing a Google Cloud project.

== Axial Hierarchy Protocol Markers

{axhp*} markers appear at the
{axl_detail_site}
of an
{axi_protocol}
as standalone annotations, paralleling the
{axho*}
operation hierarchy markers.
A protocol's structural detail — the roster of transactions, and within each transaction the call envelope and return envelope — is expressed through a four-level nesting of these markers.

=== Marker Terms and Lookahead Arities

[cols="<,^,<",options="header"]
|===
|Marker |Lookahead |Reads

|{axhpb_protocol}   |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The protocol this detail site describes (no dimensions — dimensions are at {axl_definition_site} only)
|{axhpt_transaction}|0                            |A named call/return pairing within the protocol; bare marker, identity carried through heading and prose
|{axhpc_call}       |0                            |The caller-side envelope of an enclosing transaction; bare marker, members listed in following prose
|{axhpr_return}     |0                            |The callee-side envelope of an enclosing transaction; bare marker, members listed in following prose
|===

[[axhpb_protocol]]
{axhpb_protocol}::
Opens the protocol hierarchy block at the
{axl_detail_site}.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the protocol this detail site describes.
The referenced attribute must correspond to an anchor annotated with
{axi_protocol}
at the
{axl_definition_site}.
No dimensions on this marker — all dimensions (transport, lifecycle, concurrency) are authoritative at the
{axl_definition_site}
only.

The surface syntax:

[source,asciidoc]
----
//axhpb_protocol
{apcswp_wire}

//axhpt_transaction
discerner — main detection transaction.

//axhpc_call
caller payload members:
----

[[axhpt_transaction]]
{axhpt_transaction}::
Marks a named call/return pairing within an
{axi_protocol}
detail site.
No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
A transaction groups exactly one
{axhpc_call}
and one
{axhpr_return}
that together constitute a single caller-initiated exchange.
Transaction identity is carried through section heading and prose; no linked term is expected on the following line.
Multiple
{axhpt_transaction}
blocks within one protocol describe the full set of exchanges the protocol supports.

[[axhpc_call]]
{axhpc_call}::
Marks the caller-side envelope of an enclosing
{axhpt_transaction}.
No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
The prose under this marker enumerates the envelope's members, each referencing a type term defined at the
{axl_definition_site}
(in the parent spec or a shared vocabulary).
Exactly one
{axhpc_call}
per
{axhpt_transaction}.

[[axhpr_return]]
{axhpr_return}::
Marks the callee-side envelope of an enclosing
{axhpt_transaction}.
No lookahead required — bare marker; content follows directly.
The prose under this marker enumerates the envelope's members using the same type-reference pattern as
{axhpc_call}.
Exactly one
{axhpr_return}
per
{axhpt_transaction}.
A transaction where the caller expects no data back still declares
{axhpr_return}
(with prose noting "empty envelope" or equivalent) — the return presence is the signal, and uniform structure keeps the hierarchy complete.

=== Nesting Rules

Protocol hierarchy markers establish implicit scope through document order:

* {axhpb_protocol}
opens the protocol block; all subsequent markers belong to it until document end or next
{axhpb_protocol}
* {axhpt_transaction}
must appear within
{axhpb_protocol}
scope
* {axhpc_call}
and
{axhpr_return}
must appear within
{axhpt_transaction}
scope, each exactly once
* Transactions appear in document order; no required ordering is imposed, but convention places readiness or probe transactions first and main-workload transactions second

=== Cross-Document Validation

* {axhpb_protocol}
attribute reference must match a protocol defined with
{axi_protocol}
at the
{axl_definition_site}
* {axhpb_protocol}
carries no dimensions — all dimensions are authoritative at the
{axl_definition_site}
only
* Types referenced in
{axhpc_call}
and
{axhpr_return}
member prose must resolve to
{axt_type}
anchors in the parent spec or a shared vocabulary

== Axial Voicing Entity Terms

Three annotation families enable entity specification across
{axl_definition_site_s}
and
{axl_detail_site_s},
paralleling the regime and operation annotation patterns.

Entity specifications describe objects with both structure (fields) and behavior (methods).
Methods within an entity may be *scoped* or *elevated*:

* A *scoped* method is defined entirely within the entity subdocument.
It has no parent-document linked term — its identity exists only within the entity's contract, expressed through position and prose.

* An *elevated* method possesses a linked term at the
{axl_definition_site}
in the parent document, voiced with
{axvo_method}.
Elevated methods earn independent addressability when other spec layers (procedures, dispatch protocols) need to reference them.

A well-constrained entity is its own documentation.
Scoped methods are the default; elevation is the exception for methods that must be independently addressable.

=== Definition-Site Voicing Annotations (axve_*)

{axve_*} annotations appear at the
{axl_definition_site}
between anchor and definition, declaring that the term defines an entity.

[[axve_entity]]
{axve_entity}::
Voices an
{axo_entity}
definition.
The annotated term defines an object with both structure and behavior whose contract is elaborated in a subdocument via
{axhe*}
hierarchy markers.
The first
{mcm_attribute_reference}
is the entity being defined.
+
{axd_dimension_s}:
optional
{axd_internal}.

=== Standalone Hierarchy Markers (axhe*_)

Hierarchy markers appear in entity subdocuments as standalone annotations, NOT between anchor and definition.
Each marker uses the prefix-discriminated form.
Markers with non-zero arity read ahead a specified number of
{mcm_attribute_reference_s}
from the following text.
Zero-arity markers carry their type and dimension information entirely on the annotation line.

The entity hierarchy uses a two-layer structure:

* *Layer 1*: Entity scope opened by
{axheb_entity},
containing field markers
({axheft_typed_field},
{axhefm_motif_field})
and method markers
({axhems_scoped_method},
{axheme_elevated_method}).

* *Layer 2*: Method scope opened by a method marker, containing parameter markers
({axhempt_typed_parameter},
{axhempa_arg_parameter},
{axhempm_motif_parameter})
and output markers
({axhemot_typed_output},
{axhemom_motif_output}).

Variant suffixes identify the source of type identity:

* `_typed_*` — 1-arity; lookahead reads a project-specific type term
* `_arg_*` — 1-arity; lookahead reads a shared argument term
* `_motif_*` — 0-arity; type expressed through axial motif terms on the annotation line

==== Marker Terms and Lookahead Arities

[cols="<,^,<",options="header"]
|===
|Marker |Lookahead |Reads

|{axheb_entity}             |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The entity this subdoc describes
|{axheft_typed_field}       |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A field's type (domain type term)
|{axhefm_motif_field}       |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
|{axhems_scoped_method}     |1 {mcm_inlay}                |Implementation method name; opens Layer 2
|{axheme_elevated_method}   |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |The parent-document method term
|{axhempt_typed_parameter}  |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A parameter's type (domain type term)
|{axhempa_arg_parameter}    |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |A parameter's identity (shared argument term)
|{axhempm_motif_parameter}  |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
|{axhemot_typed_output}     |1 {mcm_attribute_reference}  |An output's type (domain type term)
|{axhemom_motif_output}     |0                            |Type on annotation line (motif terms)
|===

[[axheb_entity]]
{axheb_entity}::
Opens the entity hierarchy block at the
{axl_detail_site}.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the entity this detail site describes.
The referenced attribute must correspond to an anchor annotated with
{axve_entity}
at the
{axl_definition_site}.

[[axheft_typed_field]]
{axheft_typed_field}::
Marks a data member of the entity whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— must resolve to an existing type anchor.
{axd_dimension_s}
on the annotation line qualify the field:
{axd_required}
or
{axd_optional},
{axd_repeated},
etc.

[[axhefm_motif_field]]
{axhefm_motif_field}::
Marks a data member of the entity whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
0-arity — no lookahead.
{axd_dimension_s}
on the annotation line include a type motif
({axt_string},
{axt_boolean},
{axt_array},
etc.) and structural qualifiers
({axd_required},
{axd_repeated},
etc.).

[[axhems_scoped_method]]
{axhems_scoped_method}::
Opens a scoped method scope within the entity.
The method has no parent-document
{mcm_quoin}
— its identity is an
{mcm_inlay}
that exists only within the entity subdocument.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_inlay}
— the implementation method name, recognized by its prefix pattern.
Opens Layer 2 scope for parameter and output markers.

[[axheme_elevated_method]]
{axheme_elevated_method}::
References an elevated method within the entity — a method that has a linked term at the
{axl_definition_site}
in the parent document, annotated with
{axvo_method}.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— the parent-document method term.
Opens Layer 2 scope for parameter and output markers.

[[axhempt_typed_parameter]]
{axhempt_typed_parameter}::
Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— must resolve to an existing type anchor.
{axd_dimension_s}
on the annotation line qualify the parameter:
{axd_required}
or
{axd_optional},
etc.

[[axhempa_arg_parameter]]
{axhempa_arg_parameter}::
Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose identity reuses a shared argument term.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— must resolve to an existing shared argument anchor.

[[axhempm_motif_parameter]]
{axhempm_motif_parameter}::
Marks a parameter within the current method scope whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
0-arity — no lookahead.
{axd_dimension_s}
on the annotation line include a type motif and structural qualifiers.

[[axhemot_typed_output]]
{axhemot_typed_output}::
Marks an output of the current method scope whose type identity reuses an existing domain type term.
Lookahead: 1
{mcm_attribute_reference}
— must resolve to an existing type anchor.

[[axhemom_motif_output]]
{axhemom_motif_output}::
Marks an output of the current method scope whose type is expressed entirely through axial motif terms on the annotation line.
0-arity — no lookahead.
{axd_dimension_s}
on the annotation line include a type motif and structural qualifiers.

==== Nesting Rules

Entity hierarchy markers establish implicit scope through document order:

* {axheb_entity}
opens the entity block; all subsequent markers belong to it until document end
* {axheft_typed_field}
and
{axhefm_motif_field}
must appear within
{axheb_entity}
scope but outside any method scope (Layer 1)
* {axhems_scoped_method}
and
{axheme_elevated_method}
must appear within
{axheb_entity}
scope (Layer 1); each opens a new method scope (Layer 2)
* A method marker implicitly closes any prior method scope
* {axhempt_typed_parameter},
{axhempa_arg_parameter},
{axhempm_motif_parameter},
{axhemot_typed_output},
and
{axhemom_motif_output}
must appear within a method scope (Layer 2)
* Field markers should precede method markers within the entity (fields then methods)

==== Cross-Document Validation

* {axheb_entity}
attribute reference must match an entity defined with
{axve_entity}
at the
{axl_definition_site}
* {axheme_elevated_method}
attribute reference must match a method defined with
{axvo_method}
at the
{axl_definition_site},
and that method's affiliated entity must match the entity opened by
{axheb_entity}

== Axial Type Universal Terms

{axtu_*} types represent generic infrastructure patterns that are not specific to any cloud provider or platform.
These types capture critical gestalts that appear across many systems.
Platform-specific nuances (e.g., path separators on Windows vs Unix) are handled through
{axl_voicing_s}.

[[axtu_string]]
{axtu_string}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a generic bounded string value.
{axl_voicing_s}
specify minimum and maximum length constraints appropriate to their context.

[[axtu_path]]
{axtu_path}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a file system path.
Platform differences (path separators, root conventions) are handled by
{axl_voicing_s}.
May be absolute or relative depending on context.

[[axtu_ipv4]]
{axtu_ipv4}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing an IPv4 address in dotted-decimal notation.
Format: four octets 0-255 separated by dots (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).

[[axtu_cidr]]
{axtu_cidr}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a CIDR block for network addressing.
Format: IPv4 address followed by slash and prefix length (e.g., `10.0.0.0/8`).

[[axtu_port]]
{axtu_port}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a network port number.
Valid range: 1-65535. Well-known ports are 1-1023.

[[axtu_domain]]
{axtu_domain}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a DNS domain name.
Must start and end with alphanumeric characters; may contain dots and hyphens.

[[axtu_sha256]]
{axtu_sha256}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a SHA-256 cryptographic hash.
Format: 64 lowercase hexadecimal characters.

[[axtu_xname]]
{axtu_xname}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a cross-context safe identifier.
Must start with a letter and contain only letters, digits, underscores, and hyphens.
Safe for use in file names, environment variables, and most programming contexts.

== Axial Type Google Terms

{axtg_*} types represent Google Cloud Platform resource identifiers and patterns.
These types have specific format requirements enforced by GCP APIs.

[[axtg_project_id]]
{axtg_project_id}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud Platform project identifier.
Format: 6-30 lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens; must start with a letter and end with letter or digit.
Globally unique across all of GCP.

[[axtg_region]]
{axtg_region}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud Platform region code.
Format: lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens (e.g., `us-central1`, `europe-west1`).
Determines geographic location for regional resources.

[[axtg_service_account]]
{axtg_service_account}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud Platform service account email.
Format: `{account-id}@{project-id}.iam.gserviceaccount.com` where account-id is 6-30 characters.
Used as identity for programmatic API access.

[[axtg_billing_account]]
{axtg_billing_account}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud Platform billing account identifier.
Format: `XXXXXX-XXXXXX-XXXXXX` (three groups of six uppercase alphanumeric characters).
Links projects to payment sources.

[[axtg_project_number]]
{axtg_project_number}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud Platform project number.
Format: numeric identifier automatically assigned at project creation.
Globally unique across all of GCP.
Immutable once assigned.

== Axial Infrastructure Google Terms

{axig_*} terms represent Google Cloud Platform infrastructure patterns.
Each term explicitly declares its level:

* **Service-level**: the managed platform itself
* **Instance-level**: a particular resource within a service
* **Operational**: execution patterns and states

=== Service-Level Terms

[[axig_resource_manager]]
{axig_resource_manager}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing Google Cloud Resource Manager.
Service-level: the platform for managing organizations, folders, and projects.

[[axig_cloud_storage]]
{axig_cloud_storage}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing Google Cloud Storage.
Service-level: the object storage platform, not buckets within it.

[[axig_artifact_registry]]
{axig_artifact_registry}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing Google Artifact Registry.
Service-level: the artifact management platform, not repositories within it.

[[axig_cloud_build]]
{axig_cloud_build}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing Google Cloud Build.
Service-level: the build execution platform, not individual builds.

[[axig_iam]]
{axig_iam}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing Google Cloud IAM.
Service-level: the identity and access management platform.

=== Instance-Level: Resource Manager

[[axig_organization]]
{axig_organization}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud organization.
Instance-level: a particular organization, top of GCP resource hierarchy.

[[axig_folder]]
{axig_folder}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud folder.
Instance-level: an organizational container within an
{axig_organization}.

[[axig_project]]
{axig_project}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud project.
Instance-level: a resource container with billing, APIs, and IAM.

[[axig_lien]]
{axig_lien}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Google Cloud lien.
Instance-level: a deletion protection on a resource.

=== Instance-Level: Cloud Storage

[[axig_bucket]]
{axig_bucket}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Cloud Storage bucket.
Instance-level: an object container with ACLs and lifecycle policies.

=== Instance-Level: Artifact Registry

[[axig_artifact_repository]]
{axig_artifact_repository}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing an Artifact Registry repository.
Instance-level: container for container images or generic artifacts.

[[axig_container_image]]
{axig_container_image}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a container image in Artifact Registry.
Instance-level: an image with tags and digest.

[[axig_generic_artifact]]
{axig_generic_artifact}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a generic artifact in Artifact Registry.
Instance-level: a non-container artifact (archive, binary, metadata).

=== Instance-Level: Cloud Build

[[axig_build]]
{axig_build}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Cloud Build execution.
Instance-level: a particular build processing instructions to create artifacts.

[[axig_build_trigger]]
{axig_build_trigger}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Cloud Build trigger.
Instance-level: a configured trigger that dispatches builds in response to events or API calls.

[[axig_build_config]]
{axig_build_config}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a build configuration file.
Instance-level: a committed YAML or JSON file defining build steps, images, and artifacts.

=== Instance-Level: Cloud Build v2

[[axig_cbv2_connection]]
{axig_cbv2_connection}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Cloud Build v2 connection.
Instance-level: an authenticated bridge from GCP to a GitHub organization via the "Google Cloud Build" GitHub App, with credentials stored in Secret Manager.

[[axig_cbv2_repository]]
{axig_cbv2_repository}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Cloud Build v2 repository resource within a connection.
Instance-level: a binding that grants Cloud Build access to a specific external repository.

// RETIRED: axig_repo_link — replaced by axig_cbv2_repository (2026-03-02)

=== Instance-Level: Build Provenance

[[axig_slsa_provenance]]
{axig_slsa_provenance}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing SLSA build provenance.
Instance-level: a cryptographically signed attestation recording build inputs, environment, and outputs per the SLSA framework.

=== Instance-Level: IAM

[[axig_service_account]]
{axig_service_account}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a GCP service account.
Instance-level: a non-human identity for API authentication.

[[axig_role]]
{axig_role}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a GCP IAM role.
Instance-level: a named collection of permissions.

[[axig_binding]]
{axig_binding}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a GCP IAM policy binding.
Instance-level: association of identity to role on a resource scope.

[[axig_service_account_key]]
{axig_service_account_key}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a service account key file.
Instance-level: JSON credential for service account authentication.

=== Operational Terms

[[axig_lro]]
{axig_lro}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a Long Running Operation.
Operational: async pattern returning handle for later completion check.

[[axig_project_state]]
{axig_project_state}::
A
{axl_motif}
representing a project lifecycle state.
Operational: conditions like ACTIVE, DELETE_REQUESTED describing status.

lastPrompt: Oh you are too eager to invent when the roots are already present for this.  Study much more closely, I do not permit you to indulge in this when the linter buildout is so distant from now.  All that…

content.text:
I've now read MCM in full and all of AXLA's families — control, environment, operation, type, record, JSON, argument, interface, dimension, format, regime (definition-site `axvr_*` + detail-site `axhr*`), the structural terms, protocol markers, entity voicings (`axve_`/`axhe*`), and the universal/Google type families — plus the RBS0 slices. Scope honesty: that's the grammar in full, but still essentially one RBS *subdoc* (RBSDY) checked against it, so my conformance-breadth read is N≈1.

Here is what's there — and where it shows I was about to build over existing roots.

## The correction first: source↔spec linkage already exists, and runs the other way

I proposed minting a source-side breadcrumb annotation — "the source-side analog of `//axl_voices`." That was inventing over a root that's already load-bearing. The binding between an operation and its implementation is **the shared name**, expressed three ways the lexicon already defines:

- **Quoin display text *is* the implementing symbol.** RBS0:133 — `:rbtgo_director_yoke: <<rbtgo_director_yoke,rbfl_yoke>>` — renders as `rbfl_yoke`. The operation's own section heading (RBS0:1259, `=== {rbtgo_director_yoke}`) titles itself with the bash function name. Spec→source is the rendered name.
- **`axvo_tabtarget`** binds an operation to its on-disk launcher, reading the literal filename as an `mcm_sprue` (one quoin per tabtarget).
- **`axhems_scoped_method`** binds an entity method to its implementation name as an `mcm_inlay` — "recognized by its prefix pattern."

So linkage is **by name identity**, validated by the cross-document rules — not by a free-text comment. That recasts the audit's "concept→quoin breadcrumb gap": the audit measured "does the bash cite the RBS doc," but AXLA's standard is "does the implementing symbol match its quoin's voiced rendering, and is the operation's interface surface voiced." A different, already-rooted question — and one checkable from the existing mechanism, no new annotation system. My breadcrumb idea was redundant.

## The architecture, as I now hold it

Everything hangs on the **two-site spine**: the `axl_definition_site` (parent doc — anchor + voicing annotation + definition text; establishes identity, role, positional relationships) and the `axl_detail_site` (subdoc or inline — opened by a block marker, filled with subordinate markers). Four families instantiate that one spine identically:

| Family | Definition-site voicing | Detail-site markers |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | `axvo_procedure / method / group / tabtarget` | `axhob` → `axhos`, `axhoc`, `axhop*`, `axhoo*`, `axhoq`, `axhog` |
| Regime | `axvr_regime / variable / group / kindle / validate / …` | `axhrb` → `axhrv`, `axhrgb`, `axhrgc`, `axhrgv`, `axhro_*` |
| Entity | `axve_entity` | `axheb` → fields (`axhef*`) / methods (`axhem*`), two-layer |
| Protocol | `axi_protocol` | `axhpb` → `axhpt` → `axhpc` / `axhpr` |

Each carries a **Marker / Lookahead-arity / Reads** table, **Nesting Rules**, and **Cross-Document Validation**. That triad, plus the `== Compliance Rules` section, *is* the linter specification — the "machine-readable manifest" I floated as a nudge is mostly just extracting what these tables already state.

## The graded type model I "discovered" is the lexicon's spine, everywhere

The `_typed_ / _arg_ / _motif_` triad is pervasive — operation parameters (`axhopt_/axhopa_/axhopm_`), outputs (`axhoot_/axhoom_`), entity fields (`axheft_/axhefm_`), entity params (`axhempt_/axhempa_/axhempm_`). `_typed_` is 1-arity (points at a real type quoin — `axtu_*`, `axtg_*`, `axt_insignia`…); `_motif_` is 0-arity (type carried on the annotation line, zero minting). That **is** your "language-independent type model, possible but not required at every place" — except sharpened to *always marked, formality graded by tier*. Not my idea; the grammar's core.

## What this does to the RBSDY drift

It's now precisely diagnosable against existing rules, not new ones: `//axhopt_typed_parameter` → `{rbtr_director}` violates the marker's own contract (lookahead must resolve to a **type** anchor; a role is not one), and `«STAMP»` — a textbook `axt_insignia axd_temporal` — is unvoiced. Also worth checking: the catalog's `//axvo_method axd_transient axd_grouped` requires the 2nd definition-text attribute-ref to be the affiliated `axo_entity` and the 3rd the `axo_group`; at RBS0:1259-1264 the first linked term is `{rbrv_regime}` with `{rbtgog_depot}` as group — whether `{rbrv_regime}` is the intended entity is exactly what *Operation Voicing Annotation Completeness* would adjudicate, and it reads under-conformant.

---

So: I no longer need to invent. The roots — two-site spine, name-identity linkage, graded `_typed_/_arg_/_motif_` tiers, the arity/nesting/cross-doc rules as a linter spec, `axk_premise` + `axhoq`/`axhog` as tradeoff/condition homes — are all already present, and most of what I'd have "created" is either an extraction of those tables or a redundant parallel.

I'm holding all nudge thoughts until you're satisfied I understand. When you are, the genuine seams I noticed are narrow — I'd put them in three buckets: (1) the audit's tell-2 standard vs AXLA's actual name-identity linkage; (2) a couple of spots where existing rules have no stated lookahead *scanning* rule (how a parser skips prose/blank lines between marker and its attribute-refs); (3) whether `axhopt_typed_parameter` should ever accept an inline insignia vs always a minted type quoin. One line each, not yet elaborated.

Where would you like to point me — is my read of what's there complete enough, or is there a section/seam you want me to go deeper on first?

message.content: The art of this heat will be in deft adjustment, not wholesale invention.  I can only carry out this heat if it is mechanical refinement, i.e. something we trust you-as-opus-4.8 in the future to carry out in a consistency improving way.  I can't lend enough attention to do reengineering, but I do want your help standing up the constraints to cause this activity to be health improving rather than hallucination-bait.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — and the sharpest way I can put it is that the thing we're building the constraint *against* is the failure I just demonstrated two turns ago: handed an open-ended "improve health" task, I reached for invention. So the membrane has to be built around the agent's reflex, not around the specs. That's the recursion the ROE names — the agent is an ungovernable edge, and the constraint is the containment.

Here's the litmus I'd put at the center of the whole heat:

**A mutation is licensed only where a *wrong* version of it is cheaply caught. Everywhere else, the agent's verb is detect-and-report, never repair.**

That single test sorts every candidate scrub into "safe to do now" vs "flag for the operator," and it's exactly what converts consistency-work from hallucination-bait into something you can hand a future Opus 4.8 unattended.

The constraint primitives that fall out of it — and each grounds in a root that already exists, not a new mechanism:

- **Detect-by-default, repair-by-exception.** The lexicon already models this: `mcm_link_validation` is explicitly *advisory, no modification* — "repairs are performed manually or through targeted actions." So the safe posture isn't new; it's the existing curation stance. The agent produces a worklist of violations against existing rules; it mutates only the narrow class where repair is deterministic.

- **Closed-world (no minting).** Every permitted move consumes only existing names/constants/quoins/motifs. If a fix would require minting a term, a voicing, or a type — it is *out of scope by definition* and becomes a flagged item, never an action. This is the heat's own "deft adjustment, not wholesale invention" promoted to a hard precondition.

- **Authority declared, never inferred.** An alignment move (two sites that should agree) is permitted only when the canonical side is named *in advance*. The agent aligns toward declared truth; it never guesses which side is right. If authority can't be named, it's detect-only.

- **A countable fixpoint as "Done."** Each move-type completes at "violation count → 0 (or → all-flagged)," never "better." The rbrv.env sweep already has this shape: *every path-construction resolves through the constant; count of bare literals → 0.* An agent can't invent its way to a lower count.

- **Verifier-gated mutation — which is your now/later line, derived rather than asserted.** Code-side moves have a live verifier today (shellcheck + fast suite), so a wrong repair dies at the gate → safe to mutate **now**. Spec-side moves have *no* verifier yet (the linter is distant), so a wrong repair would survive → **detect-and-report only**, repair deferred until the linter backstops it. That's the principled reason the RBS retrofit waits and the code sweeps proceed — not scheduling, but "we only mutate where a mistake can't survive."

And the part that should reassure you most: **you've already prototyped this regime inside the heat.** ₢BbAAB (the vocabulary-eviction mechanism) is precisely a table-driven, self-proving checker — rows of declared rules, a mechanical scan, a zero-violations assertion, and a self-test row proving the checker catches a violation *and* respects a keep-context before it's pointed at anything real. That self-test row is the keystone: it's how the checker earns trust without your attention. Generalize that one engine and the rbrv sweep is just its first landed row.

Which reframes what the ACG actually *is*. Not a style guide, and not prose about crispness — it's **the catalog of mechanical, verifier-backed move-types**, each one a row the engine can check and a future instance can execute toward a countable zero. The grammar (AXLA) defines correctness; the engine enforces it; the ACG names the moves and the agent operates strictly inside that table. No room to hallucinate because there's no open horizon to hallucinate toward — only counts to drive down and a flagged pile to hand back to you.

Does that litmus — *mutate only where a wrong move is cheaply caught; detect-and-report everywhere else* — match what you're reaching for? If it does, the natural next step is small: pin down the **first two or three move-types** precise enough to be engine-rows (the magic-string sweep is one proven already; name-identity match between quoin display-text and implementing symbol is a strong second, detect-only for now), and let those define the ACG's spine by example rather than by manifesto. But I'll hold there — does the framing land before we name moves?

content.text:
Good. Proceeding inside the regime — defining the first move-types as engine-rows, then *running* two of them as detectors to prove the posture produces worklists, not edits. I'll mutate nothing.

First, the two live detections (independent, so in parallel): the **name-identity** move (code-side — does each operation quoin's display-text name a real implementing function?) and the **typed-parameter conformance** move (spec-side, detect-only — does each `//axhopt_typed_parameter` lookahead point at a type?).

input.command:
cd() { return 1; }
R=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster
SPEC=$R/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc
echo "=== MOVE: name-identity (rbtgo_ operation quoins → implementing function) ==="
echo "Scope: rbtgo_* operation quoins in RBS0 mapping section only (other op prefixes rbtoe_/rbtgog_ NOT scanned)."
names=$(grep -E '^:rbtgo_[a-z_]+:' "$SPEC" | grep -oE ',[a-zA-Z0-9_]+>>' | tr -d ',>' | sort -u)
total=0; ok=0; drift=0
for n in $names; do
  total=$((total+1))
  if grep -rqE "^${n}\(\)" "$R/Tools/rbk" --include='*.sh'; then
    ok=$((ok+1))
  elif grep -rqE "(^|[[:space:]])${n}[[:space:]]*\(\)" "$R/Tools/rbk" --include='*.sh'; then
    ok=$((ok+1)); echo "  ok*   $n   (indented/nonstd def site)"
  else
    drift=$((drift+1)); echo "  DRIFT $n   (no function definition found in Tools/rbk)"
  fi
done
echo "---- name-identity: total=$total conformant=$ok drift=$drift ----"

cd() { return 1; }
R=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster
echo "=== MOVE: typed-parameter conformance (detect-only; spec-side has no verifier yet) ==="
echo "Each //axhopt_typed_parameter must look ahead to a TYPE anchor. Listing marker + lookahead target across all RBS subdocs."
grep -rn -A1 '//axhopt_typed_parameter' "$R"/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS*.adoc \
  | grep -E '\.adoc[:-][0-9]+[:-]' \
  | grep -vE '//axhopt_typed_parameter|^--' \
  | sed -E "s#$R/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/##"
echo "---- raw worklist above; type-vs-nontype classification is the linter's job, but role (rbtr_*) / group (rbtgog_*) targets are visibly non-type ----"

content.text: Found it — the file has two display-text shapes (function names like `rbfl_abjure`, but also human labels like `Image Audit` at line 136-137), and there are **39** `rbtgo_` entries, not 1. My first detector's extraction silently collapsed — exactly the kind of quiet checker failure that would poison an unattended worklist. Rewriting it cleanly (pure-bash extraction, label-vs-symbol split) and re-running with a sanity count:

input.command:
cd() { return 1; }
R=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster
SPEC=$R/Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc
echo "=== MOVE: name-identity (rbtgo_ operation quoin display-text → implementing function) ==="
total=0; ok=0; drift=0; label=0
while IFS= read -r line; do
  disp="${line##*,}"; disp="${disp%%>>*}"
  total=$((total+1))
  case "$disp" in
    *' '*) label=$((label+1)); echo "  LABEL  $disp   (human label, not a symbol — separate move-type)"; continue ;;
  esac
  if grep -rqE "(^|[[:space:]])${disp}[[:space:]]*\(\)" "$R/Tools/rbk" --include='*.sh'; then
    ok=$((ok+1))
  else
    drift=$((drift+1)); echo "  DRIFT  $disp   (no function definition found in Tools/rbk)"
  fi
done < <(grep -E '^:rbtgo_[a-z_]+:' "$SPEC")
echo "---- name-identity: total=$total  conformant=$ok  label=$label  drift=$drift ----"
echo "(sanity: total should be ~39, matching the mapping-entry count)"

content.text: Now trustworthy (total=39 ✓). But the regime says I triage the worklist with the same discipline — a "DRIFT" line is a *candidate*, not a verdict. Before I'd ever report these as actionable, I have to rule out detector false-positives and out-of-scope lanes. Verifying the four:

cd() { return 1; }
R=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster
for n in rbfd_kludge rbfd_enshrine rbfc_plumb rbld_augur; do
  echo "## $n"
  echo "  def-any-form:"; grep -rnE "(^|[[:space:]]|function[[:space:]])${n}[[:space:]]*\(\)" "$R/Tools/rbk" --include='*.sh' | head -3
  echo "  total occurrences in Tools/rbk: $(grep -rE "${n}" "$R/Tools/rbk" --include='*.sh' | wc -l | tr -d ' ')"
done

content.text:
Done — several steps in, and the most important result is the *arc*, not the worklists. Here's what happened, because it's the regime proving itself in miniature.

## The regime caught its own failure modes, live

I ran two detectors. The typed-parameter one worked first try. The name-identity one **returned `total=1` — silently broken** (my extraction collapsed on the file's two display-text shapes). A sanity count against a known quantity (39 mapping entries) caught it before its output could mislead. I fixed it, re-ran, got `total=39`. Then its 4-item "DRIFT" list triaged down to **exactly one** genuine, in-scope, still-detect-only finding. A naive agent would have "fixed" all four — including renaming a false-positive and stomping on ₣BH's in-flight work. The litmus and the triage rules filtered every one of those. That arc is the strongest evidence the framing holds.

## The first three move-types, as engine-rows

| Move | Authority (ground truth) | Mutate now? | Verifier | Done = |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **magic-string → constant** | the constant's value (string-equal) | **yes** | shellcheck + fast suite | bare path-literals → 0 (prose excluded) |
| **name-identity** (op-quoin display-text ↔ impl symbol) | *undeclared* + no verifier | no — **detect-only** | none yet (linter, distant) | every display-text resolves or is flagged |
| **typed-parameter conformance** (`//axhopt_typed_parameter` → a *type*) | AXLA marker contract | no — repair needs intent | none yet | every lookahead classified; non-types flagged |

Row 1 is the proven rbrv sweep. Rows 2–3 are detect-only *now* — exactly the verifier-gated line: code-side mutates because a wrong move dies at the test gate; spec-side reports because nothing yet catches a wrong move.

## Worklist A — typed-parameter (spec-side, detect-only)

18 sites, 17 point at type/identity anchors, **1 outlier**: `RBSDY:16 → {rbtr_director}` (a role, not a type). Clean single-item flag, caught by a one-line detector.

## Worklist B — name-identity (39 entries → triaged)

33 conformant, 2 human labels (`Image Audit`, `Image Rekon` — a separate move-type), 4 drift-candidates, triaged:

- **`rbfd_kludge` → real drift, settled area.** No such symbol anywhere; the implementation is `rbfk_kludge()` (in `rbfk_kludge.sh`). The spec display-text is stale. **Flag for you** — authority is yours to declare (my read: spec is the stale side), and it's spec-side so detect-only regardless.
- **`rbfc_plumb` → detector false-positive.** 16 references but no literal `()` definition — almost certainly dispatch-routed (`buc_execute rbfc_…`), which my literal-def check can't see. *Not drift.* This is a finding about the **detector**, not the spec.
- **`rbfd_enshrine`, `rbld_augur` → out of scope (moving lanes).** Enshrine is mid-retirement (RBSAE self-declared stale, owned by a ₣BH-adjacent pace); `rbld_augur` is in ₣BH's actively-moving Lode lane, which this heat is explicitly gated away from. Flag as owned-elsewhere; do not touch.

Net: of four candidates, **one** is a real, in-scope finding — and it still doesn't get auto-repaired.

## What this stood up, and what it taught

Three things the exercise hardened into rules for the catalog:
1. **A checker must pass a known-answer sanity test before its output is trusted** — empirically, not as theory. That's precisely ₢BbAAB's self-test-row requirement, now motivated by a real near-miss.
2. **A worklist item is a candidate, not a verdict.** False-positive refinement, authority declaration, and scope-gating are *part of each move-type's definition*, not cleanup.
3. **The false-positive class is the gating work for trustworthiness.** The name-identity detector can't be an unattended engine-row until it recognizes dispatch-routed verbs (the `rbfc_plumb` case).

The natural next step, and what I'd do: **refine the name-identity detector to resolve dispatch-routed verbs**, which closes the only false-positive class and turns row 2 from "demonstrated" into "trustworthy enough to hand off." Alternatives, one line each: **(a)** run the magic-string detector across the *other* config filenames (`rbrn`/`rbra`/`rbro`/`rbrd`.env) to size the next mutate-now sweep; **(b)** capture the litmus + these three rows into the heat now (the ACG-as-move-catalog reframing of ₢BbAAA) before going further.

I'll hold here rather than auto-capture into the heat. Which next step?

lastPrompt: I like your framing, I think you are on the right track.  Please proceed a few steps now.

message.content: Hold on, I think this got single document specific when I was looking for deft nudges to improve ACG given the findings and the aspiration for clean up, with a little bit of leeway to improve AXLA/MCM if needed.  Reframe on that

content.text:
You're right — I dropped from the nudge altitude down into one document's worklist. Reset to the right unit: **deft nudges to the ACG, earned by the findings, with a couple of candidate AXLA/MCM touches where the findings exposed a real seam.** The detectors were only ever evidence; here's what they buy us.

## Nudges to the ACG (corrections/sharpenings of the ₢BbAAA shape, not new mechanism)

1. **Fix the central linkage claim — and retract my own bad instinct.** The ACG's "reference the home" for *concepts* is already realized by **name-identity**: the implementing symbol *is* the link (quoin display-text = function name, tabtarget filename = sprue, scoped method = inlay). So the ACG should say "make the name match the quoin," **not** "add a `# see RBSxx` comment." A breadcrumb comment becomes the *exception*, reserved for where no single symbol can carry the link — composed artifacts, multi-spec modules (the real `Contract: RBSCJ` case) — and only then names spec + anchor. This kills the breadcrumb-grammar I was about to invent two turns ago.

2. **The ACG is a move-type catalog, not prose about crispness.** Its operational content is rows of (detect rule · authority · mutate-now-or-detect-only · verifier · countable Done). The "three-homes-by-when-read" frame stays as the *why*; the catalog is the *what*.

3. **Verifier-gated licensing as the ACG's one hard rule:** mutate only where a wrong move is cheaply caught; detect-and-report everywhere else. This is what gives the code-now / spec-later split a principled home instead of being a schedule.

4. **Triage belongs *inside* each move's definition**, not after it: the false-positive class to exclude, the authority that must be declared before any alignment, and the scope-gate to non-moving lanes. The name-identity run made this concrete — four candidates, one real, because those three filters did their job.

5. **Self-test-before-trust.** A checker must pass a known-answer sanity check before its output is trusted (my detector returned `total=1` and only a count caught it). The docket for ₢BbAAB already wants that engine named as the canonical checker home — this finding just hands it the motivating near-miss.

## AXLA/MCM — the "if needed" leeway

- **AXLA, genuinely needed (high confidence):** state the lookahead *scanning* rule. AXLA gives every marker its arity ("reads N attribute-refs") but never says how a parser skips intervening prose/blank lines to find them. One sentence in the structural-terms section ("skip blank lines and non-reference prose; count only `{…}` refs / backtick sprues") is the single deftest thing that makes the eventual linter buildable. Pure clarification of intent, zero new mechanism.

- **AXLA, candidate (medium confidence, held lightly):** make name-identity *decidable* by stating that an `axvo_procedure`/`axvo_method` quoin's display-text voices its implementing symbol. Right now 37/39 operation display-texts are symbols and 2 are human labels (`Image Audit`, `Image Rekon`) — that 2-of-39 split reads as drift, but the move can't *prove* it's drift until the convention is declared. This is the seam between "name-identity is a nice convention" and "name-identity is a checkable move." I'd raise it, not mint it, until you weigh in.

- **Retracted:** my earlier "register a source-breadcrumb prefix in MCM." Withdrawn — name-identity already carries the linkage; a new annotation prefix would be inventing over a working root.

## Where I'd land first

The two deftest, highest-leverage: **ACG nudge 1** (the linkage correction — it prevents the guide from institutionalizing a hallucination) and **AXLA nudge A** (the scanning-rule sentence — true gap, one sentence, unblocks the linter aspiration). Everything else can follow as the catalog fills in.

Want me to capture this nudge-set into the heat — and if so, as a reslate of ₢BbAAA toward the move-catalog framing, or as a paddock note recording the linkage correction + the AXLA seams — or keep refining the set first?

# jjezs_reslate ₢BbAAA
## Character
Doc authoring from a design settled in conversation — transcription, not invention. The paddock carries the spine, the three-homes frame, the v1 posture, and the two source-doc forms; this docket carries the operational form and the corrections that conversation produced.

## What the ACG is (operational form)
A catalog of mechanical, verifier-backed move-types — not a prose style guide. Each move states: detect-rule, authority (ground truth), mutate-now-or-detect-only, verifier, countable Done. The ACG codifies the heat's cleanup regime as this catalog; the three-homes frame stays the why.

## Cinched (deltas to the paddock spine)
- Concept linkage is NAME-IDENTITY — the implementing symbol is the link (function name = quoin display-text; tabtarget filename = sprue; scoped method = inlay). NOT a source-to-spec breadcrumb comment. A breadcrumb is the exception, only where no single symbol carries the link (composed artifacts, multi-spec modules), and then it names spec + anchor.
- Licensing: mutate only where a wrong move is cheaply caught. Code-side (resolves through a constant; shellcheck + fast suite verify) mutates now; spec-side is detect-and-report until the linter exists.
- Triage lives inside each move definition: false-positive class excluded, authority declared before any alignment, scope gated to non-moving lanes.
- A checker self-tests against a known answer before its output is trusted; the ACG names the eviction/conformance engine as the canonical home for such checks.

## Seed the catalog
- magic-string to constant (proven via the rbrv sweep) — mutate-now.
- name-identity (operation-quoin display-text vs implementing symbol) — detect-only.
- typed-parameter lookahead resolves to a type — detect-only.

## AXLA/MCM nudges in scope (deft, only if needed)
- AXLA: state the lookahead scanning rule (skip prose and blank lines; count only attribute-refs and backtick sprues).
- AXLA candidate (raise, do not mint unprompted): declare that axvo_procedure / axvo_method display-text voices the implementing symbol, making name-identity decidable.

## Done when
ACG exists as the veiled move-catalog (guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG, reachable from BCG/RCG), proprietary and not released at MVP; the mint is registered (acronym map + CLAUDE.md guide registry); the seed move-types are carried with the licensing, triage, and self-test rules stated.

# jjezs_paddock ₣Bb

## Shape

Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene, feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP feature
finalization. Each pace carries a targeted blast radius that merges trivially
into final feature work — no cross-cutting refactors. A seed, not a tree: scope
stays tight to the source/spec allocation family (magic-string -> constant
sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, source-documentation standards). Resist
becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The ACG (Allocation Coding Guide) spine: don't recreate inline what has a named
home; reference the home. Values -> constants; concepts -> quoin-refs. MCM builds
the named homes; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather than
recreate, and where prose with no spec-home actually goes.

Three homes by when-read: design-time -> spec, edit-time -> source comment
(operational mechanics only — language idiom + Pale/foreign-boundary),
execution-time -> runtime announcement. Most conceptual comments are temporal
misallocation — design-time or execution-time knowledge dumped into the
edit-time medium, where it rots for lack of a forcing function.

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation, barely touches asciidoc; that is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG. Not released at
  MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practices, state the spine. Do NOT mandate
  universals ("every verb must announce") — those are candidates, confirmed
  pace-by-pace. No qualify-enforcement in v1.
- Two source-doc forms are distinct and both blessed: contract header (bounded
  comment, edit-time) vs intent announcement (runtime printout, execution-time).
  Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-param-shows-options practice, live
  in rbfc_require_vessel_sigil) is blessed alongside happy-path announcement.

## Sequencing & lanes

The ACG guide and the eviction mechanism are heat-internal and buildable now, no
cross-heat dependency. The vocabulary work splits by who owns the terms:

- Eviction (removing superseded terms) is gated on ₣BH — the lode-capture heat
  curates the KILL/KEEP list and retires the terms; the mechanism pace builds the
  standing theurge engine, and its rows populate per-cluster behind ₣BH's
  cutovers. ACG names that engine as the canonical home for evicted-term
  assertions (ACG eating its own dog food — a named home, referenced not
  recreated).
- Cluster repair (curating living vocabulary to a single home) is disjoint from
  eviction and never removes terms. The slated noun clusters are BH/BZ-safe in
  their terms but file-gated on ₣BH, since their descriptions share rbz_zipper.sh
  and the doc_briefs ₣BH is rewriting.

Deliberately not slated until their owning heats land: reliquary / Lode / Hallmark
vocabulary (₣BH's lane), Manor / Depot / account-role vocabulary (₣BZ's lane,
citizen-model), and verb clusters (₣BH curates its own verb descriptions during
cutover). Slating against terms two hot heats are still moving would only
re-create the drift this heat exists to kill.

## Done looks like

ACG v1 exists and is reachable from BCG/RCG; the rbrv.env literal has a single
named home in both bash and rust as the first worked application of the
magic-string discipline.

## Shape

finalization. Each pace carries a targeted blast radius that merges trivially
into final feature work — no cross-cutting refactors. A seed, not a tree: scope
stays tight to the source/spec allocation family (magic-string -> constant
sweeps, decision-detail hoisting, source-documentation standards). Resist
becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The ACG (Allocation Coding Guide) spine: don't recreate inline what has a named
home; reference the home. Values -> constants; concepts -> quoin-refs. MCM builds
the named homes; ACG governs source's obligation to reference rather than
recreate, and where prose with no spec-home actually goes.

Three homes by when-read: design-time -> spec, edit-time -> source comment
(operational mechanics only — language idiom + Pale/foreign-boundary),
execution-time -> runtime announcement. Most conceptual comments are temporal
misallocation — design-time or execution-time knowledge dumped into the
edit-time medium, where it rots for lack of a forcing function.

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation, barely touches asciidoc; that is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG. Not released at
  MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practices, state the spine. Do NOT mandate
  universals ("every verb must announce") — those are candidates, confirmed
  pace-by-pace. No qualify-enforcement in v1.
- Two source-doc forms are distinct and both blessed: contract header (bounded
  comment, edit-time) vs intent announcement (runtime printout, execution-time).
  Failure-path option disclosure (the missing-param-shows-options practice, live
  in rbfc_require_vessel_sigil) is blessed alongside happy-path announcement.

## Move discipline

Every cleanup pace obeys one litmus: mutate only where a *wrong* move is cheaply
caught; everywhere else the verb is detect-and-report, never repair. Code-side
moves (the value resolves through a named constant; shellcheck and the fast suite
verify) may mutate now; spec- and document-side moves are detect-and-report until
the lexer/linter exists.

Three rules ride that litmus:

- Triage is part of every move, not an afterthought: exclude the false-positive
  class, declare the authoritative side before any alignment, and gate scope to
  settled lanes — never terms a hot heat is still moving.
- A checker proves itself against a known answer before its output is trusted;
  the eviction/conformance engine is the canonical home for such checks.
- Concept linkage is name-identity — the implementing symbol is the link (a
  function name equals its quoin's display-text; a tabtarget filename is a sprue;
  a scoped method is an inlay). A source-to-spec comment is the exception,
  reserved for where no single symbol can carry the link.

## Sequencing & lanes

The ACG guide and the eviction mechanism are heat-internal and buildable now, no
cross-heat dependency. The vocabulary work splits by who owns the terms:

- Eviction (removing superseded terms) is gated on ₣BH — the lode-capture heat
  curates the KILL/KEEP list and retires the terms; the mechanism pace builds the
  standing theurge engine, and its rows populate per-cluster behind ₣BH's
  cutovers. ACG names that engine as the canonical home for evicted-term
  assertions (ACG eating its own dog food — a named home, referenced not
  recreated).
- Cluster repair (curating living vocabulary to a single home) is disjoint from
  eviction and never removes terms. The slated noun clusters are BH/BZ-safe in
  their terms but file-gated on ₣BH, since their descriptions share rbz_zipper.sh
  and the doc_briefs ₣BH is rewriting.

Deliberately not slated until their owning heats land: reliquary / Lode / Hallmark
vocabulary (₣BH's lane), Manor / Depot / account-role vocabulary (₣BZ's lane,
citizen-model), and verb clusters (₣BH curates its own verb descriptions during
cutover). Slating against terms two hot heats are still moving would only
re-create the drift this heat exists to kill.

## Done looks like

ACG v1 exists and is reachable from BCG/RCG; the rbrv.env literal has a single
named home in both bash and rust as the first worked application of the
magic-string discipline.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BbAAA
## Character
Doc authoring from a design settled in conversation — transcription, not invention. The paddock carries the spine, the three-homes frame, the v1 posture, the two source-doc forms, and the heat's move discipline; this docket carries only the ACG's operational form.

## What the ACG is (operational form)
A catalog of mechanical, verifier-backed move-types — not a prose style guide. Each move states: detect-rule, authority (ground truth), mutate-now-or-detect-only, verifier, countable Done. The ACG codifies the heat's move discipline (see paddock) as this catalog; the three-homes frame stays the why.

## Seed the catalog
- magic-string to constant (proven via the rbrv sweep) — mutate-now.
- name-identity (operation-quoin display-text vs implementing symbol) — detect-only.
- typed-parameter lookahead resolves to a type — detect-only.

## AXLA/MCM nudges in scope (deft, only if needed)
- AXLA: state the lookahead scanning rule (skip prose and blank lines; count only attribute-refs and backtick sprues).
- AXLA candidate (raise, do not mint unprompted): declare that axvo_procedure / axvo_method display-text voices the implementing symbol, making name-identity decidable.

## Done when
ACG exists as the veiled move-catalog (guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG, reachable from BCG/RCG), proprietary and not released at MVP; the mint is registered (acronym map + CLAUDE.md guide registry); the seed move-types are carried, with the move discipline's licensing, triage, and self-test rules referenced from the paddock.

content.text:
Both parts landed. ₣Bb now has a clean single home for the discipline:

- **Paddock (`59c74bea`)** — new **## Move discipline** section carries the litmus (mutate only where a wrong move is cheaply caught; detect-and-report otherwise), plus triage-in-every-move, self-test-before-trust, and name-identity-as-linkage. Every cleanup pace in the heat now inherits these, not just the ACG-authoring one.
- **₢BbAAA docket** — trimmed to only the ACG's operational form (move-catalog, the three seed move-types, the two AXLA nudges, done-when); it now points at the paddock for the discipline instead of restating it.

The discipline lives in exactly one place and the docket references it — the heat practicing its own single-home / name-identity ethos. I also caught and removed the one stray descriptive **"regime"** that had slipped into the prior docket ("codifies the heat's cleanup regime" → "the heat's move discipline (see paddock)").

State of play: the ACG's *design* is fully captured across paddock + docket, but the **guide file itself still doesn't exist** — authoring it is the mount of ₢BbAAA, a larger step you haven't asked for yet. I'll hold here. When you want it, the next move is mounting ₢BbAAA to write the catalog; or if you'd rather keep sharpening the move-set or take the AXLA scanning-rule nudge first, say which.
